column1: Front
column2: Back

column1:
مرحبا
(mar7aba)
column2:
Hello
Example:
مرحبا، كيفك اليوم؟
mar7aba, keefak el‑yom?
Hello, how are you today?
Think of “marhaba” as a warm “marshmallow” hello – the 7 reminds you of the Arabic ح sound.

column1:
مرحبتين
(marHabtein)
column2:
Hello (in reply)
Example:
مرحبتين! كيف حالك؟
marHabtein! keef 7aalak?
Hello to you! How are you?
Saying “marHabtein” literally means “two hellos” – imagine you’re sending twice the warmth back.

column1:
مراحب
(maraaHib)
column2:
Greetings! (Hello to many)
Example:
مراحب يا جماعة!
maraaHib ya jama3a!
Greetings, everyone!
“MaraaHib” is a plural greeting – think of it as saying “many hellos.”

column1:
أهلين
(ahlein)
column2:
Hi! / “Two families”
Example:
أهلين يا صديقي!
ahlein ya Sadee’i!
Hi, my friend!
“Ahlein” literally means “two welcomes,” implying you’re so welcome you belong to two families.

column1:
أهلا و سهلا
(ahlan wa sahlan)
column2:
Welcome!
Example:
أهلا وسهلا في بيتنا.
ahlan wa sahlan fi baytna.
Welcome to our home.
Literally “easy and wide,” think of opening your house wide so guests feel at ease.

column1:
سلام عليكم
(salaamu ʿalaykum)
column2:
Peace be upon you
Example:
سلام عليكم يا جماعة!
salaamu ʿalaykum ya jama3a!
Peace be upon you, everyone!
Use this formal greeting to share “peace” – it earns instant respect.

column1:
و عليكم سلام
(wa ʿalaykum salaam)
column2:
And peace be upon you (reply)
Example:
و عليكم سلام، كيفك؟
wa ʿalaykum salaam, keefak?
And peace be upon you; how are you?
Replying with “wa ʿalaykum salaam” reflects the greeting back – like returning a smile.

column1:
سلام
(salaam)
column2:
Peace / Hi
Example:
سلام، شو الأخبار؟
salaam, shoo el‑akhbar?
Hi, what's new?
“Salaam” is both peace and hello – think of saying “peace” as your greeting.

column1:
صباح الخير
(sabaH el‑kheyr)
column2:
Good morning
Example:
صباح الخير يا حبيبتي.
sabaH el‑kheyr ya Habeebti.
Good morning, my dear.
“SabaH” sounds like “sabah”—imagine greeting the morning sun shining like gold (“kheyr”).

column1:
صباح النور
(sabaH el‑noor)
column2:
Good morning (reply)
Example:
صباح النور يا أصدقائي!
sabaH el‑noor ya aSdiqa'i!
Bright morning to you, my friends!
Respond with “noor” (light) – you’re saying “morning of light” to match “good morning.”

column1:
مساء الخير
(masa’ el‑kheyr)
column2:
Good evening
Example:
مساء الخير للجميع.
masa’ el‑kheyr lil‑jamee3.
Good evening to everyone.
“Masa’” means evening; pair it with “kheyr” (goodness) like the morning greeting.

column1:
مساء النور
(masa’ el‑noor)
column2:
Good evening (reply)
Example:
مساء النور يا سيدتي.
masa’ el‑noor ya sayyidati.
Evening of light, ma’am.
Like “sabaH el‑noor,” answer with “light” to make the greeting shine.

column1:
يعطيك العافية
(yaaʿTeek el‑ʿaafi)
column2:
May God give you health (well‑done)
Example:
يعطيك العافية على الشغل.
yaaʿTeek el‑ʿaafi ʿala esh‑shughl.
Thanks for your hard work; may God bless your health.
Say this to someone working; imagine handing them a wellness “gift.”

column1:
الله يعافيك
(Allah yaʿafeek)
column2:
May God bless you too (reply)
Example:
الله يعافيك، شكرا!
Allah yaʿafeek, shukran!
May God bless you too, thanks!
Respond back with this phrase when someone says “yaaʿTeek el‑ʿaafi.”

column1:
ألو؟
(alo?)
column2:
Hello? (telephone)
Example:
ألو؟ مين معي على الخط؟
alo? meen maʿi ʿala el‑khaTT?
Hello? Who’s on the line?
“Alo” sounds like “hello” – use it when answering the phone.

column1:
شكرا
(shukran)
column2:
Thank you
Example:
شكرا على مساعدتك.
shukran ʿala musaaʿadtak.
Thank you for your help.
“Shukran” is used everywhere – think of saying “shucks, thanks!”

column1:
شكرا كتير
(shukran kteer)
column2:
Thanks a lot
Example:
شكرا كتير على الهدية!
shukran kteer ʿala el‑hadiyyeh!
Thank you so much for the gift!
Add “kteer” (a lot) to intensify your gratitude.

column1:
ألف شكر
(alf shukur)
column2:
Thanks a thousand!
Example:
ألف شكر على مساعدتك.
alf shukur ʿala musaaʿadtak.
A thousand thanks for your help.
Literally “one thousand thanks,” use this when you’re extra grateful.

column1:
شكرا إلك
(shukran ilak/ilek)
column2:
Thanks to you
Example:
شكرا إلك على كل شي.
shukran ilak ʿala kul shi.
Thanks to you for everything.
Add “ilak/ilek” (“to you”) when you want to direct your gratitude to someone specific.

column1:
عفوا
(ʿafwan)
column2:
You’re welcome / Excuse me
Example:
عفوا، ما فهمت السؤال.
ʿafwan, ma fhimt as‑su'al.
Excuse me, I didn’t understand the question.
“ʿAfwan” doubles as “you’re welcome” and “sorry” depending on context – a handy all‑rounder.

column1:
على راسي
(ʿala raasi)
column2:
My pleasure (literally “on my head”)
Example:
شكرا على المساعدة. – على راسي!
shukran ʿala el‑musaaʿadeh. – ʿala raasi!
Thanks for the help. – My pleasure!
Imagine balancing someone’s request on your head to show you’re honored to help.

column1:
لو سمحت
(law samaHt / samaHti)
column2:
Please / Excuse me
Example:
لو سمحت، وين الحمام؟
law samaHt, weyn el‑hammam?
Excuse me, where is the bathroom?
Use this polite phrase whenever you need to get someone’s attention.

column1:
من فضلك
(min faDlak / faDlek)
column2:
Please / From your favor
Example:
كوب ميّ، من فضلك.
koob mayy, min faDlak.
A glass of water, please.
Literally “from your favor,” think of politely asking someone to grant you a favor.

column1:
أنا آسف
(ana aasif / aasife)
column2:
I’m sorry
Example:
أنا آسف على التأخير.
ana aasif ʿala at‑ta’kheer.
I’m sorry for the delay.
Use “ana aasif” to apologize; think of saying “I’m as‑if” I could change it.

column1:
مع اذنك
(maʿa iznak / iznek)
column2:
With your permission
Example:
مع اذنك، بدي مرّ.
maʿa iznak, biddi marr.
With your permission, I’d like to pass.
Politely ask for someone’s permission before doing something – it softens requests.

column1:
نعم
(naʿam)
column2:
Yes
Example:
نعم، بحكي عربي.
naʿam, baHki ʿarabi.
Yes, I speak Arabic.
“Naʿam” is formal; use it for polite or clear affirmations.

column1:
أيوه
(eywa)
column2:
Yeah / Yes (casual)
Example:
أيوه، جاهز!
eywa, jaahiz!
Yeah, I’m ready!
More laid‑back than “naʿam,” use “eywa” with friends or informal situations.

column1:
لا
(la)
column2:
No
Example:
لا، ما في مشكلة.
la, ma fi mushkila.
No, there’s no problem.
“La” is short and sharp – an easy way to refuse or negate.

column1:
يعني
(yaʿani)
column2:
I mean / like / you know
Example:
يعني، ما عندي وقت.
yaʿani, ma ʿandi waqt.
Like, I don’t have time.
People use “yaʿani” as a filler – just like “you know” or “I mean.”

column1:
تمام
(tamam)
column2:
Okay / Fine
Example:
تمام، خلينا نروح.
tamam, khalliina nrooH.
OK, let’s go.
“Tamam” is universal – think of giving a thumbs‑up and saying “ta‑mam!”

column1:
طيب
(tayyib)
column2:
Fine / Good / Well
Example:
طيب، شو نعمل؟
tayyib, shoo naʿmil?
Fine, what do we do?
“Tayyib” literally means “good” – use it to agree or move on.

column1:
طبعا
(tabʿan)
column2:
Of course
Example:
طبعا، بنقدر نساعدك.
tabʿan, binqdar nisaaʿdak.
Of course, we can help you.
Emphasize certainty by using “tabʿan” – it stresses that something is obvious.

column1:
أكيد
(akeed)
column2:
Definitely / Sure
Example:
أكيد، رح أجي.
akeed, raH aaji.
Definitely, I will come.
“Akeed” expresses certainty – as if there’s no doubt at all.

column1:
أبدا
(abdan)
column2:
Never / Not at all
Example:
أبدا ما بنسى هذا اليوم.
abdan ma bansa haada elyom.
I will never forget this day.
Emphasize negation by adding “abdan” – absolutely not.

column1:
إن شاء الله
(inshaa’ Allah)
column2:
God willing / Hopefully
Example:
رح نزورك بكرا، إن شاء الله.
raH nzoorak bukra, inshaa’ Allah.
We will visit you tomorrow, God willing.
Always add “inshaa’ Allah” when talking about the future – it shows humility.

column1:
كيفك؟
(keefak / keefek)
column2:
How are you? (to a male/female)
Example:
مرحبا! كيفك اليوم؟
mar7aba! keefak el‑yom?
Hi! How are you today?
“Keefak” (to a man) and “keefek” (to a woman) literally mean “how’s your state?”

column1:
كيف كل شي؟
(keef kul shee?)
column2:
How’s everything?
Example:
كيف كل شي معك؟
keef kul shee maʿak?
How’s everything with you?
“Kul shee” means “everything”; use this for a general check‑in.

column1:
شو الأخبار؟
(shoo el‑akhbar?)
column2:
What’s new? / What’s the news?
Example:
شو الأخبار عندك؟
shoo el‑akhbar ʿandak?
What’s new with you?
Ask for “akhbar” (news) to see what’s going on in someone’s life.

column1:
الحمد لله
(al‑Hamdu lillah)
column2:
Praise be to God (I’m fine)
Example:
كيفك؟ – الحمد لله.
keefak? – al‑Hamdu lillah.
How are you? – I’m fine, praise God.
Use this humble answer instead of boasting – you’re giving thanks rather than bragging.

column1:
أنا منيح
(ana mneeH / mneeHa)
column2:
I’m good / fine
Example:
أنا منيح، شكرا!
ana mneeH, shukran!
I’m good, thank you!
“MneeH” (good) has the sound of “meh‑neeh” – smile as you say it.

column1:
ما شي حال
(ma shee Haal)
column2:
It’s OK / Things are alright
Example:
كيفك؟ – ما شي حال.
keefak? – ma shee Haal.
How are you? – Things are all right.
Use this when you’re neither great nor terrible – just steady.

column1:
كل شي تمام
(kul shee tamam)
column2:
Everything’s fine
Example:
كل شي تمام عندي.
kul shee tamam ʿandi.
Everything’s fine on my end.
  Combine “kul shee” (everything) with “tamam” to reassure someone all is well.

column1:
شو اسمك؟
(shoo ismak / ismek?)
column2:
What’s your name?
Example:
مرحبا، شو اسمك؟
mar7aba, shoo ismak?
Hello, what’s your name?
“Shoo” means “what,” and “ismak/ismek” means “your name.” Easy!

column1:
اسمي...
(ismi ...)
column2:
My name is …
Example:
اسمي أحمد.
ismi Ahmad.
My name is Ahmad.
“Ismi” is literally “my name.” Insert your name right after it.

column1:
من وين أنت؟
(min weyn enta / enti?)
column2:
Where are you from?
Example:
من وين أنت؟
min weyn enta?
Where are you from?
“Weyn” means “where,” so you’re literally asking “from where you?”

column1:
أنا من…
(ana min …)
column2:
I’m from …
Example:
أنا من فلسطين.
ana min Falasteen.
I’m from Palestine.
Just like English, say “ana min…” and add your country or city.

column1:
أديش عمرك؟
(addeesh ʿomrak / ʿomrek?)
column2:
How old are you?
Example:
أديش عمرك؟
addeesh ʿomrak?
How old are you?
“ʿUmr” means “age/life,” and “addeesh” means “how much” – literally “how much is your age?”

column1:
عمري…
(ʿomri …)
column2:
I am … years old
Example:
عمري ثلاثين سنة.
ʿomri thalaatheen saneh.
I am thirty years old.
To answer, start with “ʿomri” and add the number and “saneh” (years).

column1:
شو بتشتغل؟
(shoo btashtaghl / btashtagheli?)
column2:
What do you do? (job)
Example:
شو بتشتغل؟
shoo btashtaghl?
What’s your job?
“Btashtaghl” comes from “shaGHal” (work) – you’re asking “what do you work?”

column1:
شو عملك؟
(shoo ʿamlak / ʿamlek?)
column2:
What’s your work? (job)
Example:
شو عملك بهالشركة؟
shoo ʿamlak bi hayy shirké?
What’s your job in this company?
“ʿAmal” means work – you’re asking “what is your work?”

column1:
أنت ساكن وين؟
(enta saaken weyn? / enti saakneh weyn?)
column2:
Where do you live?
Example:
أنت ساكن وين؟
enta saaken weyn?
Where do you live?
“Saaken” means living – you’re asking literally “you live where?”

column1:
أنا ساكن ب…
(ana saaken bi … / ana saakneh bi …)
column2:
I live in …
Example:
أنا ساكن ببيروت.
ana saaken bi Bayroot.
I live in Beirut.
Answer with “ana saaken bi” plus your city or neighborhood.

column1:
تشرفنا
(tasharrafna)
column2:
Nice to meet you
Example:
تشرفنا يا أستاذ.
tasharrafna ya ustaadh.
Nice to meet you, sir.
Literally “we were honored” – say this when meeting someone new.

column1:
شرف إلي
(sharaf ilee)
column2:
The honor is mine (reply)
Example:
تشرفنا – شرف إلي.
tasharrafna – sharaf ilee.
Nice to meet you. – The honor is mine.
Use this gracious reply to return the compliment when someone says “tasharrafna.”

column1:
فرصة سعيدة
(fursa saʿeedeh)
column2:
Happy chance / Nice to meet you
Example:
فرصة سعيدة يا محمد.
fursa saʿeedeh ya Muhammad.
Happy chance, Mohammed.
Literally “happy opportunity” – use it to express delight at meeting someone.

column1:
أنا أسعد
(ana asʿad / asʿad)
column2:
I’m happier (reply)
Example:
فرصة سعيدة. – أنا أسعد.
fursa saʿeedeh. – ana asʿad.
Happy chance. – I’m happier.
Reply politely that you’re even happier to meet the person.

column1:
أديش؟
(addeesh?)
column2:
How much?
Example:
أديش هذا؟
addeesh haada?
How much is this?
A simple, one‑word question for price or quantity – very handy when shopping.

column1:
أديش حق ال…؟
(addeesh Haqq el‑…?)
column2:
What’s the price of …?
Example:
أديش حق الساندويش؟
addeesh Haqq as‑sandwish?
What’s the price of the sandwich?
Use “Haqq” (price of) plus the item to ask for its cost.

column1:
بدي أشتريه
(biddi ashtariih / ashtariha)
column2:
I want to buy this
Example:
بدي أشتريه، قديش حقه؟
biddi ashtariih, addeesh Haq‑o?
I want to buy it; how much is it?
“Biddi” means “I want,” and “ashtari” is “to buy” – together they’re self‑explanatory.

column1:
أديش الساعة؟
(addeesh as‑saaʿa?)
column2:
What’s the time?
Example:
أديش الساعة؟
addeesh as‑saaʿa?
What time is it?
“Saʿa” means hour or clock, so you’re literally asking “how much is the hour?”

column1:
بتعرف وين الحمام؟
(btaʿrif / btaʿrfi weyn el‑hammam?)
column2:
Do you know where the bathroom is?
Example:
لو سمحت، بتعرف وين الحمام؟
law samaHt, btaʿrif weyn el‑hammam?
Excuse me, do you know where the bathroom is?
“Hammam” is “bathroom,” so just remember to ask politely!

column1:
بتحكي إنجليزي؟
(btiHki inglizi?)
column2:
Do you speak English?
Example:
بتحكي إنجليزي؟
btiHki inglizi?
Do you speak English?
“Ing-lizi” sounds like “English” – easy to remember.

column1:
مو فاهم عربي كويس
(mu faahim / faahime ʿarabi kwayyis)
column2:
I don’t understand Arabic well
Example:
مو فاهم عربي كويس، ممكن تحكي شوي شوي؟
mu faahim ʿarabi kwayyis, mumkin tiHki shway shway?
I don’t understand Arabic well; can you speak slowly?
“Mu” is the negation – add it before “faahim” (understanding) to say you don’t understand.

column1:
ممكن تعطيني المينو؟
(mumkin taʿTiini el‑menu?)
column2:
Could you give me the menu?
Example:
ممكن تعطيني المينو، من فضلك؟
mumkin taʿTiini el‑menu, min faDlak?
Could you give me the menu, please?
“Mumkin” means “maybe/could,” and “taʿTiini” is “you give me.”

column1:
ممكن تعطيني الفاتورة؟
(mumkin taʿTiini el‑faatura?)
column2:
Could you give me the bill?
Example:
الحساب، لو سمحت.
el‑7isaab, law samaHt.
The bill, please.
“Faatura” is the invoice; ask politely when you’re ready to pay.

column1:
تفضل
(tafaddal / tafaddali)
column2:
Here you go / Please (offering)
Example:
تفضل، هذا لك.
tafaddal, haada lek.
Here you go, this is for you.
Literally “favor yourself,” use it when handing something or inviting someone in.

column1:
…، لو سمحت
(… law samaHt)
column2:
___, please (in a taxi or request)
Example:
على المحطة، لو سمحت.
ʿala el‑maHaTTa, law samaHt.
To the station, please.
Place your destination before “law samaHt” to politely direct a taxi or request.

column1:
دغري
(dughri)
column2:
Straight ahead
Example:
روح دغري، بعدين عاليمين.
rooH dughri, baʿdeen ʿal‑yameen.
Go straight, then to the right.
“Dughri” sounds like “directly” – keep going straight.

column1:
دور عاليمين
(dur ʿal‑yameen)
column2:
Turn right
Example:
دور عاليمين عند الإشارة.
dur ʿal‑yameen ʿand el‑ishaara.
Turn right at the traffic light.
“Yameen” means “right”; pair it with “dur” (turn).

column1:
دور عاليسار
(dur ʿal‑yasar)
column2:
Turn left
Example:
دور عاليسار بعد الجسر.
dur ʿal‑yasar baʿd el‑jisr.
Turn left after the bridge.
“Yasar” means “left”; pair it with “dur” to instruct a left turn.

column1:
هون لو سمحت
(hoon law samaHt)
column2:
Stop here, please
Example:
هون لو سمحت، بدي أنزل.
hoon law samaHt, biddi anzil.
Stop here please, I’d like to get out.
Use “hoon” (here) with “law samaHt” to ask a driver to stop at a specific spot.

column1:
يلا
(yalla)
column2:
Let’s go / Come on
Example:
يلا، تأخرنا!
yalla, ta’akhkharnah!
Come on, we’re late!
A versatile urging phrase – use “yalla!” to get people moving.

column1:
مبروك
(mabruuk)
column2:
Congratulations!
Example:
مبروك على الوظيفة الجديدة!
mabruuk ʿala el‑waZiifeh el‑jdiideh!
Congratulations on the new job!
Use “mabruuk” for any achievement – wedding, birth, promotion, etc.

column1:
ألف مبروك
(alf mabruuk)
column2:
A thousand congratulations!
Example:
ألف مبروك على التخرج!
alf mabruuk ʿala at‑taKharroj!
A thousand congratulations on the graduation!
If “mabruuk” isn’t enough, add “alf” for extra excitement!

column1:
كل سنة وأنت طيب
(kul sana wa enta / enti tayyib / tayybeh)
column2:
Happy birthday / Happy New Year (lit. every year and you are good)
Example:
كل سنة وأنت طيب يا صديقي!
kul sana wa enta tayyib ya Sadeeqi!
Happy birthday, my friend!
Use this phrase for birthdays, New Year, or any annual celebration.

column1:
مع السلامة
(maʿ as‑salaameh)
column2:
Goodbye / Go with safety
Example:
مع السلامة، بشوفك بكرا.
maʿ as‑salaameh, bshoofak bukra.
Goodbye, I’ll see you tomorrow.
“With safety” – send someone off wishing them safety.

column1:
وداع
(wadaaʿ)
column2:
Farewell
Example:
وداع يا صديقي العزيز.
wadaaʿ ya Sadeeqi al‑ʿaziz.
Farewell, my dear friend.
Use “wadaaʿ” for formal or solemn goodbyes.

column1:
يلا باي
(yalla bye)
column2:
Bye! / See you!
Example:
يلا باي، بشوفك قريبا.
yalla bye, bshoofak ariiban.
Bye, see you soon.
Combine “yalla” with the borrowed “bye” to end a conversation quickly.

column1:
بشوفك بكرا
(bshoofak / bshoofek bukra)
column2:
See you tomorrow!
Example:
مع السلامة، بشوفك بكرا!
maʿ as‑salaameh, bshoofak bukra!
Goodbye, see you tomorrow!
“Bshoofak/ek” means “I see you” – add “bukra” (tomorrow) to specify the time.

column1:
بتحكي عربي؟
(btiHki ʿarabi?)
column2:
Do you speak Arabic?
Example:
بتحكي عربي؟
btiHki ʿarabi?
Do you speak Arabic?
A must‑ask question for travelers; “ʿarabi” means Arabic.

column1:
أيوه، بحكي عربي
(aywa, baHki ʿarabi)
column2:
Yes, I speak Arabic
Example:
أيوه، بحكي عربي شوي.
aywa, baHki ʿarabi shway.
Yes, I speak a little Arabic.
Answer positively with “aywa” and “baHki ʿarabi” to affirm your language skills.

column1:
لأ، ما بحكي عربي
(la, ma baHki ʿarabi)
column2:
No, I don’t speak Arabic
Example:
لأ، ما بحكي عربي.
la, ma baHki ʿarabi.
No, I don’t speak Arabic.
Use “ma” (not) before the verb to form a negative statement.

column1:
فينك تساعدني؟
(feenak / feenik tisaʿdini?)
column2:
Can you help me?
Example:
فيني أطلب مساعدتك؟
feeni aTlub musaaʿadtak?
Can I ask for your help?
“Feenak tisaʿdini?” literally means “are you able to help me?” – a polite request.

column1:
وين بقدر لاقي…؟
(weyn biqdar laaqi …?)
column2:
Where can I find …?
Example:
وين بقدر لاقي تاكسي؟
weyn biqdar laaqi taxi?
Where can I find a taxi?
“Laqi” means “to find”; add what you’re looking for after it.

column1:
عندك…؟
(ʿandak / ʿandik …?)
column2:
Do you have …?
Example:
عندك واي فاي؟
ʿandak Wi‑Fi?
Do you have Wi‑Fi?
Use “ʿandak/ʿandik” to ask if someone has something – like Wi‑Fi, time, or change.

column1:
بدي…
(biddi …)
column2:
I want …
Example:
بدي قهوة.
biddi qahwa.
I want coffee.
Follow “biddi” with whatever you’d like – food, help, items, etc.

column1:
بدي أشرب
(biddi ishrib)
column2:
I want to drink
Example:
بدي أشرب مي.
biddi ishrib mayy.
I want to drink water.
“Ishrib” means “to drink”; combine with “biddi” to express thirst or desire for a beverage.

column1:
بدي أطلب
(biddi uTlob)
column2:
I want to order
Example:
بدي أطلب بيتزا.
biddi uTlob pizza.
I want to order pizza.
“Utlob” means “to request/order” – use it in restaurants or on the phone.

column1:
بدي فنجان قهوة
(biddi finjaan qahwa)
column2:
I want a cup of coffee
Example:
بدي فنجان قهوة مع حليب.
biddi finjaan qahwa maʿ Haleeb.
I want a cup of coffee with milk.
“Finjaan” is “cup”; combine it with your favorite drink for a polite order.

column1:
الحساب، لو سمحت
(el‑7isaab, law samaHt)
column2:
The bill, please
Example:
النادل، الحساب لو سمحت.
an‑naadel, el‑7isaab law samaHt.
Waiter, the bill please.
Use “el‑7isaab” (the account) when you’re ready to pay.

column1:
شو بتنصحني؟
(shoo bitansahni / bitansahiini?)
column2:
What do you recommend?
Example:
شو بتنصحني أطلب؟
shoo bitansahni aTlob?
What do you recommend I order?
Use this phrase to ask for suggestions in restaurants or any situation.

column1:
يلا ناكل
(yalla naakul)
column2:
Let’s eat!
Example:
جوعانين؟ يلا ناكل!
juʿaaneen? yalla naakul!
Hungry? Let’s eat!
Add “naakul” (we eat) to “yalla” for a playful invitation to the table.

column1:
كيف بقرأ هذا؟
(keef btaqra haada?)
column2:
How do you read/pronounce this?
Example:
كيف بقرأ هذا الكلمة؟
keef btaqra haadi el‑kalmeh?
How do you pronounce this word?
Use “keef btaqra” (how do you read) to ask for pronunciation help.

column1:
بتعرف وين لاقي هذا؟
(btaʿrif weyn laaqi haada?)
column2:
Do you know where I can find this?
Example:
بتعرف وين لاقي هذا الكتاب؟
btaʿrif weyn laaqi haada el‑ketaab?
Do you know where I can find this book?
Ask for directions to find specific items or locations.

column1:
نزلني هون
(nazzilni hoon)
column2:
Let me off here
Example:
نزلني هون لو سمحت.
nazzilni hoon, law samaHt.
Let me off here, please.
Use in taxis or buses when you want to get out at a specific spot.

column1:
كيف بقدر أساعدك؟
(keef baqdar saaʿdak?)
column2:
How can I help you?
Example:
كيف بقدر أساعدك اليوم؟
keef baqdar saaʿdak el‑yom?
How can I help you today?
Use this phrase to offer assistance; it literally means “how can I be able to help you?”

column1:
شو اسم هذا بالعربي؟
(shoo haada ismu bil‑ʿarabi?)
column2:
What is this called in Arabic?
Example:
شو اسم هذا بالعربي؟
shoo haada ismu bil‑ʿarabi?
What is this called in Arabic?
A useful question to learn vocabulary on the go – ask whenever you’re curious.

column1:
ما بفهم
(ma bifham)
column2:
I don’t understand
Example:
ما بفهم عليك.
ma bifham ʿaleek.
I don’t understand you.
Use this phrase when you’re lost in conversation – don’t be shy to say it!

column1:
أنا مبسوط إنك هون
(ana mabsuut innak hoon)
column2:
I’m happy that you’re here
Example:
أنا مبسوط إنك هون اليوم.
ana mabsuut innak hoon elyom.
I’m happy that you’re here today.
“Mabsuut” means happy – share this phrase to make someone feel welcome.

column1:
مين هي البنت؟
(meen hiyyeh el‑bint?)
column2:
Who is this girl?
Example:
مين هي البنت اللي معك؟
meen hiyyeh el‑bint illi maʿak?
Who is the girl who is with you?
“Bint” means girl/daughter – use this question to ask who she is.
